title: Master JavaScript Promise & Async Execution
description: Step through the event loop, microtask & macrotask queues, async/await suspension, and tricky async patterns. Detailed MDN-linked explanations reveal after every answer.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
The event loop runs all synchronous code first, then drains the microtask queue, and only then picks the next macrotask.

1. `"1"` logs synchronously.
2. `setTimeout` schedules a **macrotask**.
3. `Promise.resolve().then(...)` enqueues `"3"` as a **microtask**.
4. The `new Promise` executor runs *synchronously*: `resolve()` settles the promise, then `"4"` logs. The `.then` for `"5"` becomes the second microtask.
5. `"6"` logs synchronously.
6. Microtasks drain in order: `"3"`, `"5"`. Finally the macrotask logs `"2"`.

The key trap: code after `resolve()` inside an executor still runs, because `.then` callbacks never run synchronously.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
The event loop drains the **entire** microtask queue before processing the next macrotask — even microtasks that were enqueued *while* draining.

Here `p1` runs as a microtask, and completing it resolves the next promise in the chain, which enqueues `p2` as another microtask. Since the queue must be fully empty before a macrotask runs, `p2` also beats the setTimeout callback, no matter how long the promise chain is.

A common wrong intuition is that the runtime alternates one microtask, one macrotask — it does not.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
Three rules combine here: synchronous code first (`1`, `5`), then microtasks (`4`), then macrotasks ordered by when their timers expire.

The `0`ms timer (`3`) fires before the `50`ms timer (`2`) even though it was registered later — setTimeout ordering depends on expiry time, not registration order, when delays differ.

Note that `setTimeout(..., 0)` still never runs before the microtask queue is empty: the delay is a *minimum*, and the callback must wait for its turn in the event loop.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
The Promise constructor calls its executor **synchronously, during construction**. Both `executor 1` and `executor 2` log before any `.then` callback can run, because `.then` callbacks are always deferred to the microtask queue.

Once the synchronous script finishes, the two queued microtasks run in registration order: `p1 then 1`, then `p2 then 2`.

This synchronous-executor rule is why promises can wrap code that starts work immediately (like opening a connection) while still delivering results asynchronously.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
Tasks spawned from inside other tasks land in different places:

1. Sync: `1`, `6`. Queues: macrotask `[2]`, microtask `[4]`.
2. Microtask `4` runs and schedules a **new macrotask** (`5`), which goes to the *back* of the macrotask queue, behind `2`.
3. Macrotask `2` runs and schedules a **microtask** (`3`). After each macrotask, the microtask queue drains completely — so `3` runs *before* the next macrotask.
4. Finally macrotask `5` runs.

Rule of thumb: microtasks scheduled inside a macrotask run immediately after it; macrotasks scheduled inside a microtask wait their turn at the back of the line.

questions.title: Two timers are registered with the same delay. In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
Between two consecutive macrotasks, the event loop always drains the microtask queue completely.

The first timer callback logs `A` and enqueues a microtask for `B`. Even though the second timer (`C`) is already expired and waiting, the event loop checks microtasks first — so `B` runs before `C`.

This is the foundation of every "microtasks have priority" puzzle: each macrotask gets a full microtask drain *after* it, and only then does the next macrotask start.

questions.title: What is the state of `p` at the moment `console.log` reads it?

options.text: Pending — the `.then` callback has not run yet
Fulfilled with `1` — the original value before `.then`
Rejected — you cannot log a promise object

questions.explanation:
`p` is the **new promise returned by `.then`**, not the original `Promise.resolve(1)`. Even though the source promise is already fulfilled, the `.then` callback can only run as a microtask — after the current synchronous code (including the `console.log`) finishes.

At log time the callback hasn't produced a value yet, so `p` is still *pending* (Node prints `Promise { <pending> }`).

This illustrates that `then()` always returns a fresh promise whose fate is decided later, never synchronously.

questions.title: Which of these schedule work on the **microtask** queue? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: The code after `await` in an async function

questions.explanation:
Promise reaction callbacks, `queueMicrotask`, and `await` continuations all share the **same microtask queue** — an `await` literally suspends the function and schedules its continuation as a microtask.

`setTimeout`, even with a `0`ms delay, schedules a **macrotask** (a "task" in spec language). That single difference explains most ordering puzzles: all pending microtasks always run before the next `setTimeout` callback, regardless of registration order.

questions.title: Which word is logged **first**?
questions.explanation:
Synchronous code always runs to completion before *any* queued callback — that's the "run-to-completion" guarantee of the JavaScript execution model.

`setTimeout` and `.then` only *schedule* work; neither can interrupt the currently running script. So `script` logs first, then the microtask (`promise`), then the macrotask (`timer`).

Full output: `script, promise, timer`.

questions.title: When does the event loop process the microtask queue?

options.text: After the current task finishes, draining it completely before the next macrotask
It processes exactly one microtask after each macrotask
Only after all macrotasks in the queue have finished
On a fixed timer, roughly every few milliseconds

questions.explanation:
After every task — the initial script counts as one, and each macrotask callback counts as one — the engine drains the microtask queue **completely**, including microtasks that were enqueued during the drain itself.

This "drain to empty" rule is why a chain of `.then` calls fully finishes before the next `setTimeout` fires, and why infinitely re-queueing microtasks can starve the event loop entirely (no rendering, no timers).

questions.title: In what order do the values log?

options.text: `1, caught err, 3` — reject stops the executor

questions.explanation:
Calling `reject()` (or `resolve()`) inside an executor does **not** throw, return, or otherwise stop the executor — it only changes the promise's state. `"2"` still logs synchronously right after the rejection.

The `.catch` handler is a microtask, so it runs after all synchronous code, including `"3"`.

If you want to exit the executor at the rejection point, you must `return` explicitly: `return reject("err")` is a common idiom.

questions.title: A promise is resolved and rejected multiple times. What logs?
questions.explanation:
A promise can settle exactly **once**. The first call to `resolve` or `reject` locks in the state; every later call is silently ignored — no error, no warning.

Here `resolve("first")` wins, so the promise is fulfilled with `"first"` and the `.catch` never fires.

This immutability is core to the Promise design: once you hold a settled promise, its value can never change underneath you, no matter who else has a reference to the `resolve`/`reject` functions.

questions.title: What does throwing inside an executor do?

options.text: Logs `caught boom` — the throw becomes a rejection
The error crashes the script before `.catch` is attached
Logs `caught undefined` — the error object is lost

questions.explanation:
The `new Promise` machinery wraps the executor call in an implicit try/catch: a synchronous `throw` inside the executor **rejects** the promise with the thrown value, exactly as if `reject(error)` had been called.

One caveat: this only applies *before* the promise settles. If you `throw` after already calling `resolve()`, the throw is swallowed — the promise is locked to its first settlement.

This is why wrapping legacy code in a Promise constructor is safe even if it throws synchronously.

questions.title: The executor never calls `resolve` or `reject`. What logs?

options.text: `end` only — the promise stays pending forever

questions.explanation:
A promise has no built-in timeout. If the executor never calls `resolve` or `reject`, the promise remains *pending* forever, and every handler attached to it simply never runs. There is no error and no warning — `"done"` is just never logged.

Forever-pending promises are a real bug class: an `await` on one suspends that async function permanently. Defensive patterns like a `Promise.race` against a timeout promise exist precisely to guard against this.

questions.title: What happens when you construct a promise without an executor: `new Promise()`?

options.text: It returns a promise resolved with `undefined`
A `SyntaxError` is reported at parse time

questions.explanation:
The Promise constructor requires its argument to be callable. `new Promise()` (or `new Promise(42)`) throws `TypeError: Promise resolver undefined is not a function` **synchronously**, at construction time — there is no promise to attach a `.catch` to.

If you just need an already-settled promise, use the static helpers instead: `Promise.resolve(value)` or `Promise.reject(error)`. They skip the executor entirely.

questions.title: A promise is resolved with another promise. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `sync` only — resolving with a promise is a TypeError

questions.explanation:
Calling `resolve(somePromise)` does **not** fulfill the outer promise with the promise object. Instead the outer promise *adopts the state* of the inner one: it stays pending until `inner` settles, then settles the same way with the same value.

So `outer` waits ~20ms for the timer, then fulfills with the string `"inner"` — handlers never see the wrapper promise.

This recursive unwrapping is why `.then` chains and `await` always deliver plain values, never nested `Promise<Promise<...>>`.

questions.title: The resolver is captured and called later. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `before, after` — resolving outside the executor does nothing

questions.explanation:
Resolving a promise only *schedules* its handlers — it never invokes them synchronously. When `resolveFn(99)` runs, the `.then` callback is enqueued as a microtask, but the current synchronous code keeps going, so `"after"` logs first.

This guarantee (handlers are always asynchronous, even if the promise is already settled) protects you from "sometimes sync, sometimes async" callbacks — a notorious source of bugs known as releasing Zalgo.

Capturing `resolve` externally like this is the basis of the deferred pattern and `Promise.withResolvers()`.

questions.title: `Promise.resolve` receives a custom thenable. In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
A *thenable* is any object with a `then` method. `Promise.resolve` assimilates thenables — but it calls their `then` method **asynchronously**, in a microtask, never during the current synchronous run.

So `start` and `end` log first; then the engine calls `thenable.then(resolve, reject)` as a microtask (`then called`), and resolving it schedules the final handler (`value done`).

Thenable assimilation is how promises interoperate with third-party promise libraries — and it's the source of the famous "extra microtask tick" in later questions.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
Each `.then` returns a **new promise** that fulfills with its callback's return value, so the chain transforms the value step by step: `1` comes in, `2` is passed on, then `4`.

The common mistake is doubling too eagerly (`1, 4, 8`): each callback doubles its *input*, which is the previous callback's output — not some accumulated future value.

Think of a chain as a pipeline: each stage sees exactly what the previous stage returned.

questions.title: The first callback has no `return`. What logs?

options.text: `got start, next start` — the value flows through

questions.explanation:
A `.then` callback that finishes without an explicit `return` returns `undefined`, and that becomes the fulfillment value of the next promise in the chain. The original `"start"` does **not** flow through automatically.

This is one of the most common real-world promise bugs: a logging or side-effect `.then` inserted in the middle of a chain silently replaces the value with `undefined`.

If you need a passthrough side effect, return the value (`.then((v) => { log(v); return v; })`) — or use `finally`, which ignores its return value by design.

questions.title: `.then` is called with a non-function argument. What logs?
questions.explanation:
Per the spec, non-function arguments to `.then` are **silently ignored** and replaced with an identity passthrough. `then(2)` behaves like `then((v) => v)`, so `1` flows through unchanged to the final callback.

No error is thrown — which can hide bugs like `.then(handler())` (calling the handler immediately and passing its result) instead of `.then(handler)`.

The same passthrough rule is what lets a rejection skip over `.then(onFulfilled)` links until it finds a rejection handler.

questions.title: One callback returns a promise. Where does `b` land relative to the `t` chain?

options.text: `a, t1, b, t2, t3, t4` — same tick as a plain return value
`a, t1, t2, b, t3, t4` — one extra tick
`a, t1, t2, t3, b, t4` — two extra ticks
`a, b, t1, t2, t3, t4` — the returned promise resolves immediately

questions.explanation:
Returning a **promise** from a `.then` callback delays the outer chain by **two extra microtask ticks** compared to returning a plain value.

Why two? The engine must (1) call `.then` on the returned thenable to subscribe to it — that's one scheduled job — and (2) when it reports back, resolve the outer promise — a second job. Only then is the next chain callback enqueued.

If the callback had returned the string `"b"` directly, `b` would log right after `t1`. This "thenable assimilation" cost is invisible in normal code but decisive in ordering puzzles. (Verified on V8/Node.)

questions.title: Several handlers are attached to the same promise. What logs?

options.text: `then1 1, then2 2` — a promise can be consumed once

questions.explanation:
Promises are **multicast**: any number of handlers can subscribe to the same promise, and each receives the same settled value, in registration order. `p` is the promise returned by the first `.then`, so it fulfills with `2`, and both `then2` and `then3` see `2`.

Note what `p.then(...); p.then(...)` is *not*: it is not a chain. Both branches read from `p` directly — neither sees the other's return value.

This fan-out behavior makes promises safe to cache and share, unlike one-shot callbacks.

questions.title: One chain and one fork on the same promise. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `a, b` — only one branch may consume `p`

questions.explanation:
Both branches register on the already-fulfilled `p`, so the microtask queue starts as `[a, c]`. Running `a` resolves the *next* promise in its chain, which only **then** enqueues `b` — behind `c`.

This is the rule that makes parallel chains interleave: a chained callback is never pre-queued; it gets enqueued only when its predecessor's promise settles.

Distinguishing a **chain** (`p.then(...).then(...)`) from a **fork** (`p.then(...); p.then(...)`) is essential for reading ordering puzzles.

questions.title: Two independent chains. In what order do the values log?
questions.description:
```js
Promise.resolve()
  .then(() => console.log(1))
  .then(() => console.log(2));

Promise.resolve()
  .then(() => console.log(3))
  .then(() => console.log(4));
```
questions.explanation:
Independent chains **interleave** rather than running to completion one at a time. Initially the queue holds the first callback of each chain: `[1, 3]`. Running `1` enqueues `2` (behind `3`); running `3` enqueues `4` (behind `2`).

Each chain advances exactly one link per "round", because the next link is only scheduled when the previous one finishes.

This round-robin pattern is a direct consequence of the FIFO microtask queue — and a favorite interview trap.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?

options.text: `start, end, caught oops` — a rejected chain skips `.then`

questions.explanation:
Rejection handlers are microtasks like any other, so both synchronous logs (`start`, `end`) come first.

The key concept: a `.catch` handler that returns normally **recovers** the chain. Its return value (`"recovered"`) fulfills the next promise, so the following `.then` runs as if nothing had failed.

`.catch(fn)` is literally `.then(undefined, fn)` — it's just another link that maps a rejection to a new outcome.

questions.title: What does `finally` do to the value flowing through?

options.text: `cleanup, got value` — finally's return value is ignored
`cleanup, got ignored` — finally replaces the value

questions.explanation:
`finally` is for side effects: its callback receives **no arguments** and its return value is **discarded** — the original value (or rejection) passes through untouched. Here `"ignored"` is thrown away and the chain still delivers `"value"`.

There is one exception: if the `finally` callback *throws* (or returns a rejected promise), that failure replaces the passing value.

This makes `finally` ideal for cleanup like hiding a spinner — it can observe completion but cannot accidentally rewrite the result.

questions.title: Is the promise returned by `.then` the same object?
questions.explanation:
Every call to `.then` (and `.catch`, `.finally`) returns a **brand-new promise** representing the outcome of that particular handler — never the original.

This is what makes chaining work: `p2` fulfills with the *handler's* result (`2`), not with `p1`'s value (`1`). It also means attaching a `.catch` to the wrong link (`p1.catch(...)` vs `p2.catch(...)`) protects different stages of the pipeline.

Promises are immutable once settled; transformation always happens by deriving new promises.

questions.title: Which call is `promise.catch(handler)` exactly equivalent to?
questions.explanation:
`catch(handler)` is defined in the spec as a thin wrapper that calls `this.then(undefined, handler)`. The `undefined` fulfillment slot means fulfilled values pass straight through, while rejections are routed to the handler.

Understanding this equivalence demystifies chain behavior: a `.catch` is just another `.then` link that only reacts to the rejected path, and like any link it returns a new promise — which is why returning a value from `.catch` *recovers* the chain.

questions.title: The fulfillment handler throws. Which handler catches it?

options.text: `catch inside` — the same level's rejection handler cannot catch it
`second arg inside, catch inside` — both run
Nothing logs — the error is swallowed

questions.explanation:
The two arguments of a single `.then(onFulfilled, onRejected)` handle the **incoming** promise's two outcomes — they never handle each other. The source promise here is *fulfilled*, so `onFulfilled` runs; when it throws, the error rejects the **next** promise in the chain, where the downstream `.catch` picks it up.

This is the practical argument for preferring `.then(f).catch(g)` over `.then(f, g)`: a trailing `.catch` also covers errors thrown *inside* `f`, while the two-argument form does not.

questions.title: What arguments does a `finally` callback receive?
questions.description:
```js
Promise.resolve(42)
  .finally((v) => console.log("finally got", v))
  .then((v) => console.log("then got", v));
```

options.text: `then got 42` only — finally requires a zero-argument function

questions.explanation:
A `finally` callback is invoked with **no arguments** — by design it cannot know (or care) whether the promise fulfilled or rejected, only that it settled. So `v` is `undefined` inside the callback, while the original `42` continues down the chain untouched.

If your cleanup logic needs the value or the error, `finally` is the wrong tool — use `.then(onFulfilled, onRejected)` or duplicate the logic in `.then`/`.catch`.

questions.title: The `finally` callback throws. What logs?

options.text: `then data` — finally cannot affect the chain
Nothing logs — the throw is ignored like a return value

questions.explanation:
`finally` ignores its callback's **return value**, but a **thrown error** (or returned rejected promise) is different: it replaces the outcome and rejects the chain. The original `"data"` is lost, the `.then` is skipped, and the `.catch` receives the cleanup error.

The asymmetry is intentional: returning something from cleanup is almost always accidental, while throwing signals that cleanup itself failed — which callers must hear about.

This mirrors `try/finally` in synchronous code, where a `throw` in the `finally` block overrides the original result too.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?

options.text: `caught oops` — the chain ends at the catch

questions.explanation:
Throwing inside a `.then` callback rejects the promise it returns — exactly equivalent to returning `Promise.reject(error)`. The `.catch` handles it (`caught oops`) and, by returning normally (here `undefined`), produces a **fulfilled** promise.

So the final `.then` runs: a `.catch` that doesn't re-throw "heals" the chain. If you want the failure to keep propagating after logging it, you must `throw e` again inside the catch handler.

This fulfilled-after-catch behavior is the promise analogue of a `try/catch` block that swallows the exception.

questions.title: What happens to the `.then` between the rejection and the `.catch`?
questions.description:
```js
Promise.reject(new Error("e1"))
  .then(() => console.log("skipped"))
  .catch((e) => console.log("caught", e.message))
  .then(() => console.log("after"));
```

options.text: `caught e1, after` — the first `.then` never runs

questions.explanation:
A `.then` with only a fulfillment handler passes rejections through untouched (its missing `onRejected` defaults to a re-throwing passthrough). The rejection therefore "tunnels" past `skipped` straight to the first link that has a rejection handler — the `.catch`.

After the catch recovers, the chain is fulfilled again and `after` runs.

Mental model: a rejected state travels down the chain, skipping every `onFulfilled`, until something handles it — just like an exception unwinding past stack frames without `catch` blocks.

questions.title: The first `.catch` re-throws. What logs?

options.text: `first original` — a catch always ends error handling

questions.explanation:
Throwing inside a `.catch` rejects the promise that the `.catch` returns — sending the chain back into the rejected state with the **new** error. The second `.catch` therefore sees `wrapped`, not `original`.

Re-throwing is the standard way to handle an error partially (log it, translate it, wrap it with context) while still letting callers know the operation failed.

Without the second `.catch`, the `wrapped` error would become an unhandled rejection — handling an error and then throwing a new one re-creates the obligation to handle it.

questions.title: Two `.catch` links in a row. What logs?
questions.explanation:
Rejection handlers only run when the incoming promise is **rejected**. After `catch1` recovers (returns `"recovered"` without throwing), the chain is fulfilled — so `catch2`, which only has a rejection handler, passes the fulfilled value straight through without running.

The `.then` then receives `"recovered"`.

Symmetry to remember: fulfilled values skip `.catch` links exactly the way rejections skip `.then(onFulfilled)` links. Each link reacts to one path and forwards the other.

questions.title: An async function with no `await`. In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
Calling an async function runs its body **synchronously** up to the first `await` — and with no `await` at all, the entire body runs synchronously, so `foo` logs before `end`.

The only asynchronous part is the return: the value `1` is wrapped in a promise, so the `.then` callback is deferred to the microtask queue and logs last.

Takeaway: the `async` keyword alone doesn't make code run later; only `await` (or the promise machinery around the return value) introduces deferral.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
`await` is a suspension point. `bar()` runs synchronously through `b`, then `await` suspends the function and **returns control to the caller** — even though the awaited promise is already resolved.

The continuation (everything after the `await`) is scheduled as a microtask, so `d` logs before `c`.

This is the single most important async/await fact: code after `await` *never* runs synchronously, no matter how trivially settled the awaited value is.

questions.title: Both `await`s receive `null`, not a promise. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `1, 2, 3, 4` — awaiting a non-promise is a no-op

questions.explanation:
`await` works on **any** value: non-thenables are wrapped as if by `Promise.resolve(value)`, so `await null` still suspends the function for one microtask tick.

Trace: `1` logs, the first `await` suspends `main`, the outer script logs `4`; then each microtask resumption logs `2` and `3` in turn.

Every `await` is an asynchronous boundary — there is no "fast path" that keeps running synchronously for plain values. (V8 optimizes already-resolved awaits down to one tick, but never to zero.)

questions.title: Two async functions run "concurrently". What does the final log show?
questions.explanation:
Each function runs synchronously to its first `await` (`x1`, `y1`), then the outer script finishes (`sync`). From there the two functions **interleave**: their continuations alternate through the microtask queue in the order they suspended.

Drain: resume `x` → `x2` (suspends again, re-queued at the back), resume `y` → `y2`, resume `x` → `x3`.

This tick-by-tick interleaving is JavaScript's cooperative concurrency: single-threaded, but multiple async functions make progress in turns at every `await` boundary.

questions.title: Nested async calls. In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
Calls drill down synchronously: `a1`, then `b()` runs to `b1`, then `c()` runs to `c1` and returns a fulfilled promise. Now `b`'s `await` suspends `b`, which makes `a`'s `await` suspend `a`, and control returns to the script: `main`.

The unwinding then happens one microtask at a time: `b` resumes (`b2`) and completes, which fulfills the promise `a` is awaiting, so `a` resumes (`a2`) one tick later.

Nested `await`s form a chain of suspensions that resolve **inside-out**, each costing a microtask tick.

questions.title: When `a` awaits `b()`, when does `b`'s body run?

options.text: `a1, b1, c, a2` — b's body runs synchronously during the call
`a1, c, b1, a2` — await defers the call itself

questions.explanation:
In `await b()`, the expression `b()` is an ordinary function call — it evaluates **first, synchronously**. `b`'s whole body runs (logging `b1`) and returns a fulfilled promise; only *then* does `await` suspend `a`.

So the order is: `a1`, `b1` (inline), `c` (caller continues after suspension), `a2` (microtask resumption).

Common misreading: "await delays the call." It doesn't — `await` only operates on the call's *result*. The work before `b`'s own first `await` always happens immediately.

questions.title: What does calling an async function return?

options.text: `true`, then `value 7` — the return value is wrapped in a promise
A TypeError — you cannot call `.then` on a number

questions.explanation:
An async function **always** returns a promise, no matter what the body returns. `return 7` fulfills that promise with `7`; `return` with nothing fulfills it with `undefined`; a `throw` rejects it.

The caller can therefore treat any async function uniformly: `await` it or attach `.then`.

This wrapping happens even for the synchronous no-`await` case — which is why an async function can never return a raw value to its caller, by design.

questions.title: Three async functions return the same value three ways. In what order do the labels log?

options.text: `value V, t1, await A, t2, promise P, t3`
`promise P, await A, value V, t1, t2, t3`
`value V, await A, promise P, t1, t2, t3`
`t1, t2, t3, value V, await A, promise P`

questions.explanation:
Measured against the `t1/t2/t3` ruler chain (verified on V8/Node):

- `return "V"` settles immediately — its `.then` fires on **tick 1**.
- `return await promise` costs **one extra tick** (the `await` resumption) — tick 2.
- `return Promise.resolve(...)` costs **two extra ticks** — the engine must subscribe to the returned thenable and then resolve the outer promise — tick 3.

So inside async functions, `return await p` is actually *faster* than `return p` by one tick, contrary to the popular "never use return await" advice (which predates this and also loses stack traces and `try/catch` coverage).

questions.title: What happens when you use `await` inside a regular (non-async) function?

options.text: A `SyntaxError` at parse time — the code never runs
A `TypeError` at runtime when `f` is called
It works, but `v` is a promise instead of the value
It works in Node but not in browsers

questions.explanation:
`await` is only valid inside async functions (and at the top level of ES modules). In a regular function it is a **syntax error**, reported when the source is parsed — before a single line executes.

This is stricter than a runtime error: the entire script/module fails to load.

Subtlety: in non-module ("sloppy script") code outside any function, `await` parses as a plain identifier, which produces different confusing errors — one more reason modern tooling defaults to modules.

questions.title: Where is **top-level** `await` (outside any function) allowed?

options.text: At the top level of ES modules
Nowhere — `await` always requires an async function

questions.explanation:
Top-level await is an ES2022 feature of **modules**: `<script type="module">`, `.mjs` files, or packages with `"type": "module"`. The module itself behaves like a big async function, and importers wait for its awaited work before executing.

It is *not* allowed in classic scripts or CommonJS (`require`-based) files — there, `await` at the top level is a syntax error.

Use it sparingly: a slow top-level `await` delays every module that imports yours.

questions.title: Async IIFEs are passed to `Promise.all`. In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
Function arguments are evaluated **before** the function is called — so both async IIFEs execute (synchronously, having no `await`) and log `a` and `b` while building the array, before `Promise.all` even runs.

Then `await` suspends `run`, letting the outer script log `after`; the combined promise resolves and `1 2` logs last.

Lesson: passing promises *into* a combinator means the underlying work has already started. `Promise.all` doesn't start anything — it only waits.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
`await` converts a rejected promise into a **thrown exception** at the `await` expression — which a normal `try/catch` block can handle. The `console.log("value", ...)` line is skipped, just like code after a synchronous throw.

Timing still applies: the rejection is delivered via the microtask queue, so `sync` (the caller's code) logs before `caught error`.

This bridging of promises into `try/catch` is the main ergonomic win of async/await over `.then`/`.catch` chains.

questions.title: The same promise is awaited twice. What logs?

options.text: `cached, cached` — a settled promise can be awaited any number of times
`cached, undefined` — the value is consumed by the first await
`cached`, then the second await hangs forever
A TypeError — a promise cannot be awaited twice

questions.explanation:
A promise **caches its settled state forever**. The first `await` waits ~10ms for the timer; the second `await` finds `p` already fulfilled and resumes on the next microtask tick with the same value.

Promises are not streams or one-shot channels — any number of consumers (`await`s, `.then`s) can read the same result at any time, even long after settlement.

This makes promises ideal for memoization: store the promise (not the value), and every caller awaits the same in-flight or completed operation.

questions.title: Inside an async function, what number does the expression `await 5` evaluate to?
questions.explanation:
Awaiting a non-thenable wraps it as if by `Promise.resolve(5)` and then unwraps it again — so the expression evaluates to `5` itself.

The value is unchanged, but the *timing* is not: the function still suspends for one microtask tick. `await` on a plain value is therefore a (slightly wasteful) way to yield to the microtask queue.

Detail: if the value has a `.then` method (a thenable), `await` will call it instead of passing the object through — only non-thenables pass unchanged.

questions.title: The second promise finishes first. What does `Promise.all` deliver?

options.text: `fast undefined` — only settled promises are included

questions.explanation:
`Promise.all` preserves **input order** in its result array, regardless of which promise settles first. Position `0` is always `slow`'s value, position `1` always `fast`'s — completion timing only affects *when* the combined promise fulfills (here, after the slowest, ~30ms).

This guarantee is what makes destructuring like `const [user, posts] = await Promise.all([...])` safe.

If you instead need results *as they complete*, you want a different pattern (e.g. `for await` over promises or a queue), not `Promise.all`.

questions.title: One input rejects immediately. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `caught bad` — the pending promise is cancelled

questions.explanation:
`Promise.all` **short-circuits on the first rejection**: it rejects right away (microtask, so `caught bad` logs first) without waiting for the others.

But note the second line: the pending promise is *not cancelled* — JavaScript promises aren't cancellable, and the 20ms timer still fires (`ok finished`). Its result is simply ignored.

In real code this matters: "abandoned" operations keep consuming resources and running side effects. To actually stop work you need explicit cancellation, e.g. `AbortController`.

questions.title: `Promise.all` receives an empty array. What logs?

options.text: `sync, resolved []` — it fulfills with an empty array
`sync` only — with no inputs it stays pending forever

questions.explanation:
With zero inputs, the condition "all inputs have fulfilled" is vacuously true, so `Promise.all([])` fulfills **immediately** (well — on the next microtask tick, hence after `sync`) with `[]`.

This edge case makes `Promise.all(items.map(fn))` safe when `items` happens to be empty — the code proceeds with an empty result array instead of hanging.

Contrast with `Promise.race([])`, which stays pending forever — a later question covers that asymmetry.

questions.title: The input mixes plain values and promises. What logs?

options.text: A TypeError — all inputs must be promises

questions.explanation:
`Promise.all` passes every input through `Promise.resolve()`, so plain values are treated as already-fulfilled promises of themselves. The result array is `[1, 2, "three"]` — values unwrapped, order preserved.

This coercion makes mixed arrays ergonomic: you can combine cached values with in-flight requests without special-casing.

The same rule applies to `race`, `any`, and `allSettled` — all the promise concurrency methods accept any iterable of values-or-thenables.

questions.title: A fulfillment at 10ms races a rejection at 30ms. What logs?

options.text: `catch lose` — rejections always win a race

questions.explanation:
`Promise.race` settles with the **first settlement of any kind** — fulfillment *or* rejection. Here the 10ms fulfillment wins, so the race fulfills with `"win"`; the later rejection is ignored (the race is already settled and cannot change).

Had the rejection fired first, the race would reject — `race` gives no preference to either outcome, unlike `Promise.any`, which skips rejections while waiting for a fulfillment.

The ignored `lose` rejection does **not** become an unhandled rejection: `race` subscribed to it, which counts as handling.

questions.title: `Promise.race` receives an empty array. What logs?

options.text: `end, settled` — an empty race fulfills with undefined

questions.explanation:
A race settles when its first input settles — with **no inputs, nothing can ever win**, so `Promise.race([])` stays pending forever and `settled` never logs.

Note the asymmetry with `Promise.all([])`, which fulfills immediately with `[]`: "all of nothing" is trivially done, but "the first of nothing" never happens.

Forever-pending promises are silent: no error, no warning. An `await Promise.race(maybeEmpty)` is a subtle way to hang an async function — guard against empty input lists.

questions.title: The fastest promise rejects. What does `Promise.any` deliver?

options.text: `sync, caught true` — the first rejection settles it

questions.explanation:
`Promise.any` waits for the **first fulfillment** and ignores rejections along the way. The 10ms rejection doesn't settle it; the 30ms `medium` fulfillment does — `slow` arrives too late.

It only rejects if *every* input rejects (with an `AggregateError`), which doesn't happen here, so the `catch` is never reached.

Mnemonic: `race` = first **settlement** wins; `any` = first **success** wins. `any` is the right tool for "try several mirrors/endpoints, take whichever answers first".

questions.title: Every input rejects. What logs?

options.text: `false`, then `error1` — only the first rejection is kept
`true`, then `error3` — only the last rejection is kept
An unhandled rejection crashes before the catch runs

questions.explanation:
When all inputs reject, `Promise.any` rejects with an `AggregateError` whose `errors` array holds **every** rejection reason, in **input order** (not settlement order).

So the catch logs `true` and `error1,error2,error3` — no reason is lost.

`AggregateError` is the only place the language bundles multiple failures into one; if you need the same for `Promise.all`-style logic, that's what `Promise.allSettled` plus your own filtering is for.

questions.title: One of three steps fails under `Promise.allSettled`. What logs?

options.text: `ok s1, err s2 failed, ok s3`
`ok s1, ok s3` — rejected entries are omitted
`err s2 failed` — the first failure rejects everything
`ok s1, err s2 failed` — processing stops at the failure

questions.explanation:
`Promise.allSettled` waits for **every** input to settle and never short-circuits. Each result is a descriptor object: `{ status: "fulfilled", value }` or `{ status: "rejected", reason }`, in input order.

So all three log: success, failure, success — the middle rejection doesn't affect its neighbors.

Reach for `allSettled` when operations are independent and you want a complete report (e.g. batch uploads); reach for `all` when one failure makes the rest meaningless.

questions.title: Which combinator's returned promise **never rejects**?
questions.explanation:
`Promise.allSettled` always fulfills (once every input settles) with an array of status descriptors — failures are *data*, not control flow. The only theoretical exception is passing a non-iterable, which rejects with a `TypeError`.

The others can all reject: `all` on the first input rejection, `race` if the first settlement is a rejection, and `any` if **all** inputs reject (`AggregateError`).

Practical consequence: a bare `await Promise.allSettled(...)` needs no `try/catch`, but you must remember to inspect each `status` yourself.

questions.title: A 50ms operation races a 20ms timeout. What logs?
questions.explanation:
This is the classic **timeout pattern**: race the real operation against a promise that rejects after a deadline. Here the timeout (20ms) fires before the data (50ms), so the race rejects with `timeout` and the `.catch` runs. The `.then` never runs — the race settled once, permanently.

Caveats worth knowing: the losing operation is *not cancelled* (it still completes at 50ms, ignored), and the timer keeps running even on success. Production versions pair this with `AbortController` and `clearTimeout`. The platform now offers `AbortSignal.timeout()` for exactly this.

questions.title: Which combinators can settle **before** all of their inputs have settled? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: `Promise.all` — rejects on the first input rejection

questions.explanation:
Three of the four concurrency combinators can short-circuit:

- `all` gives up early on the first **rejection** (but must wait for all fulfillments).
- `race` is pure short-circuit: the very first settlement of any kind decides it.
- `any` commits early on the first **fulfillment** (but must wait through all rejections).

Only `allSettled` unconditionally waits for every input. Remember: short-circuiting settles the *combined* promise early — the remaining input promises keep running regardless.

questions.title: An async function throws. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `caught boom, sync` — the throw happens during the call
An uncaught exception stops the script before `sync`
`sync` — async throws cannot be caught with `.catch`

questions.explanation:
A `throw` inside an async function does **not** propagate synchronously to the caller — it rejects the promise the function returns. The call `f()` itself completes normally, handing back a rejected promise; the `.catch` handler then runs as a microtask, after `sync`.

So `async function f() { throw x; }` is observably equivalent to `function f() { return Promise.reject(x); }` for the caller.

This is why wrapping the *call* in `try/catch` without `await` catches nothing: the error lives in the returned promise, not the call stack.

questions.title: An async function is called without `await`. What logs (besides any runtime warning)?

options.text: `wrapper start, risky start, wrapper end, microtask` — plus an unhandled rejection
`wrapper start, risky start, wrapper end, wrapper catch fail, microtask`
`wrapper start, wrapper end, risky start, microtask`
`wrapper start, risky start, risky after, wrapper end, microtask`

questions.explanation:
Without `await`, `risky()` is **fire-and-forget**: its promise is created, dropped on the floor, and its later rejection has no handler. `wrapper` itself finishes successfully, so `wrapper().catch(...)` never fires — a `.catch` only covers the chain it's attached to.

The rejection surfaces instead as an unhandled rejection (a console error in browsers; process-crashing by default in Node).

If you intentionally don't await, still attach a handler: `risky().catch(log)` — or use `void risky()` only when the function handles its own errors.

questions.title: Can this `try/catch` (or a promise `.catch`) catch an error thrown in a timer callback?

options.text: `end`, then an uncaught error — the try/catch cannot help

questions.explanation:
The `try` block only covers the **synchronous** act of *scheduling* the timer, which succeeds instantly. By the time the callback throws, the `try/catch` frame is long gone — the callback runs later, on a fresh, empty call stack. The error becomes an **uncaught exception** (`window.onerror` / process crash), not a promise rejection, so no `.catch` can see it either.

To handle errors in timer callbacks you must put the `try/catch` *inside* the callback — or better, wrap the timer in a promise so failures become rejections that flow through normal promise channels.

questions.title: `forEach` with an async callback. In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
`forEach` is promise-blind: it calls each callback and **ignores the returned promise**. Both async callbacks start, suspend at their `await`, and `forEach` returns immediately — so `done` logs first, and the items log later as microtasks.

There is no way to make `forEach` wait. Worse, rejections inside the callbacks become unhandled.

For sequential iteration use `for...of` with `await`; for parallel, `await Promise.all(items.map(async ...))`. This `forEach` trap is one of the most common async bugs in real codebases.

questions.title: Errors are thrown, caught, and thrown again along one chain. What logs?

options.text: `catch1 err1, catch2 err2` — a second failure ends the chain

questions.explanation:
Follow the state of the chain link by link:

1. `err1` rejects → `then2` (fulfillment-only) is **skipped**.
2. `catch1` handles it and returns a value → chain fulfilled again.
3. The next `.then` runs and throws `err2` → rejected again.
4. `catch2` handles it → fulfilled → `final` runs.

A promise chain is a little state machine alternating between fulfilled and rejected tracks; each link either reacts to the current track or forwards it. Reading chains this way makes even long ones mechanical to trace.

questions.title: A rejection is "handled" by returning `null`. What logs?

options.text: `main error: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'toUpperCase')`

questions.explanation:
The `.catch(() => null)` swallows the network error and fulfills with `null` — so `main` carries on as if the fetch succeeded. The real failure then happens *downstream*: `null.toUpperCase()` throws a `TypeError`, which rejects `transform`'s promise and is finally caught by `main().catch`.

The original `network error` is gone; what surfaces is a confusing null-access error far from the root cause.

Lesson: recover with a sentinel like `null` only if the very next code checks for it. Otherwise let the error propagate — or rethrow with added context.

questions.title: An error passes through a `try/finally` with no `catch`. What logs?

options.text: An unhandled rejection — finally breaks the propagation chain

questions.explanation:
A `finally` block without a `catch` does **not** swallow the exception: it runs its cleanup, then lets the error continue propagating. So `middle finally` logs first, `middle`'s promise rejects with the original error, and `outer`'s `catch` receives it.

The ordering is guaranteed — `middle`'s promise cannot settle until `middle` finishes executing, and that includes the `finally` block.

This mirrors synchronous `try...finally` exactly: cleanup is interposed on the unwinding path without altering the outcome.

questions.title: When must a rejection handler be attached to avoid an `unhandledrejection` event?

options.text: Before the microtask queue drains at the end of the current task — option A is safe, option B is too late
Any time before the process or page exits
Within the same expression — even option A is too late
It makes no difference; the event always fires for every rejection

questions.explanation:
The runtime checks for unhandled rejections **after the current task's microtask queue drains**. A handler attached synchronously in the same script (option A) is registered in time, so no event fires. Attaching it in a later task (option B) is too late: the `unhandledrejection` event has already fired (browsers then emit `rejectionhandled` when you attach late; Node by default has already crashed the process).

Practical rule: never park a rejected (or possibly-rejecting) promise without a handler across task boundaries — attach `.catch` in the same tick you create it.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?

options.text: `catch fail, finally` — code after a failed try block is unreachable

questions.explanation:
Once `await` turns the rejection into a thrown exception, ordinary `try...catch...finally` sequencing takes over: the `catch` block handles it, the `finally` block runs next, and — because the exception was *handled* — execution continues normally after the statement (`after try`).

The function completes successfully; its promise fulfills with `undefined`.

async/await's superpower is exactly this: asynchronous failures flow through the same well-understood structured control flow as synchronous ones.

questions.title: One function throws, the other returns `Promise.reject`. What logs?

options.text: `a A, b B` — both produce rejected promises
`b B` — the throw in `a` crashes the script first
`a A` only — `b` is not async, so `.catch` is unavailable
`b B, a A` — explicit rejections settle before throws

questions.explanation:
For callers, the two are equivalent: each call returns a promise already rejected with an `Error`, and each `.catch` fires as a microtask in registration order (`a` first).

Note `b` isn't even `async` — any function that returns a promise participates in the same machinery; the `async` keyword just automates wrapping and enables `await` inside.

Style guidance: prefer `throw` inside async functions (works with `try/catch`, reads like sync code) and reserve `Promise.reject` for non-async helpers or eagerly-failing validation in promise-returning APIs.

questions.title: `queueMicrotask` and `.then` are mixed. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `E, A, C, B, D` — queueMicrotask callbacks run first
`E, B, D, A, C` — promise callbacks run first

questions.explanation:
`queueMicrotask` and promise reactions share **one** microtask queue — there is no priority between the two APIs. Callbacks run in pure registration order: `A, B, C, D`, after the synchronous `E`.

A tempting wrong model is that one mechanism "batches" before the other; in reality `Promise.resolve().then(cb)` is essentially `queueMicrotask(cb)` plus promise bookkeeping.

(Historical note: the original version of this question shipped with the "grouped by API" answer marked correct — running the code disproves it.)

questions.title: Microtasks enqueue more microtasks. In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
Initial queue after the synchronous script: `[A-callback, D]`. Running the `A` callback logs `A` and appends `B` then `C` to the **end** of the very same queue — behind the already-waiting `D`.

Drain continues in FIFO order: `D`, then `B`, then `C` — all within one drain cycle; newly added microtasks never preempt older ones, but they *do* run before any macrotask.

This "append to the live queue" behavior is what allows microtask loops to starve the event loop — covered in the next questions.

questions.title: Microtasks keep scheduling more microtasks. In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
Each microtask schedules the next one, and the event loop keeps draining until the queue is **completely empty** — so all three `micro` logs happen before the already-expired timer gets a turn.

Remove the `count < 3` guard and the program never reaches the macrotask: timers, rendering, and I/O callbacks would all be blocked forever. This failure mode is called **microtask starvation**.

It's the flip side of the priority rule from earlier questions: microtasks always win, so an unbounded supply of them means everything else loses.

questions.title: An async function competes with `queueMicrotask`. In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
Trace the queue. Sync phase: `1` (inside `main`), suspension at the first `await` enqueues `resume-main`; then the script queues `5` and logs `6`. Queue: `[resume-main, 5]`.

- `resume-main`: logs `2`, queues `3`, second `await` queues `resume-main-2`. Queue: `[5, 3, resume-main-2]`.
- `5`, then `3`, then `resume-main-2` logs `4`.

Key ordering fact: `main`'s continuation was enqueued **before** the outer `queueMicrotask(5)`, because the `await` ran first — so `2` beats `5`. An `await` continuation is just a microtask whose position depends on when the suspension happened.

questions.title: Chained `.then`s interleave with `queueMicrotask`. What does the final log show?
questions.explanation:
Initial queue: `[qm1, pt1, qm2, pt3]` — registration order. The crucial detail: `pt2` is **not** pre-queued; it's only enqueued when `pt1` runs and settles its link.

Drain: `qm1` → `pt1` (appends `pt2` at the back) → `qm2` (appends `qm3`) → `pt3` → `pt2` → `qm3`.

Result: `qm1,pt1,qm2,pt3,pt2,qm3`. Chained `.then` callbacks lag one drain-step behind everything registered up front, which is why `pt3` (registered directly on a resolved promise) beats `pt2` (second link of a chain). Verified by execution — this exact question is easy to get wrong by hand.

questions.title: In browsers, what happens to `setTimeout(fn, 0)` when timers are deeply nested?

options.text: After about 5 levels of nesting, the delay is clamped to a minimum of 4ms
Nothing — 0ms always means "as soon as possible" at any depth
Nested timers are clamped to one frame (16ms)
Browsers throw after 100 nested timers to prevent runaway loops

questions.explanation:
The HTML spec orders browsers to enforce a **4ms minimum delay once `setTimeout`/`setInterval` nesting exceeds 5 levels** — a self-rescheduling 0ms timer chain actually ticks every ~4ms. The first few levels may also be clamped to ~1ms depending on the browser. (Background tabs throttle far more aggressively — to 1s or beyond.)

This is why `setTimeout(fn, 0)` loops are poor for high-frequency work: use `queueMicrotask` (no yield to rendering), `requestAnimationFrame` (per frame), or `MessageChannel` tricks instead. Details: Reasons for delays longer than specified.

questions.title: Two timers with the identical delay. In what order do they fire?

options.text: `first, second` — equal delays fire in registration order
`second, first` — the most recent timer fires first
Only one fires; equal delays coalesce into a single timer

questions.explanation:
Timers with the same expiry time fire in the order their `setTimeout` calls executed — the timer list behaves FIFO for equal deadlines, and the spec guarantees this ordering.

Each callback is its own macrotask: `first` runs to completion (including draining any microtasks it spawns) before `second` starts. They do not merge or race.

This guarantee extends across different delays scheduled at different times: whichever timer *expires* earlier runs earlier, with registration order as the tie-breaker.

questions.title: A timer is cancelled right after scheduling. What logs?

options.text: `done`, then a TypeError from the cleared timer

questions.explanation:
`clearTimeout` removes the pending timer before the event loop ever gets a chance to run it — even a `0`ms timer, because the current script always finishes first, and the cancellation happens within it.

This synchronous-code-first guarantee is what makes cancel-after-schedule patterns reliable: you can always schedule a timeout and revoke it later in the same tick.

Contrast this with promises: a timer is cancellable before it fires, but a promise, once created, cannot be "un-scheduled" — only ignored.

questions.title: A 10ms timer vs. a 50ms busy-wait. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `loop done, timer` — the timer fires late, after ~50ms
`timer, loop done` — 10ms elapses during the loop, so the timer interrupts it
`loop done` only — the missed deadline cancels the timer

questions.explanation:
A `setTimeout` delay is a **minimum**, not a guarantee — and nothing can interrupt running JavaScript. The thread is busy in the `while` loop when the 10ms deadline passes; the timer callback just sits in the task queue until the script yields, so it fires after ~50ms.

This is the run-to-completion model: timers, clicks, and network callbacks all wait for the current code to finish.

It's also why long synchronous work freezes UIs, and why sub-millisecond timer precision is impossible to promise in JavaScript.

questions.title: What happens with a gigantic delay: `setTimeout(fn, 2 ** 31)`?

options.text: `sync, fired` — the delay overflows and the timer fires almost immediately
`sync` only — oversized delays are silently dropped

questions.explanation:
Timer delays are stored as **32-bit signed integers** in browsers and Node. The maximum is `2³¹ − 1` ms (~24.8 days); `2 ** 31` overflows, and the runtime falls back to a tiny delay — Node warns `TimeoutOverflowWarning` and sets it to 1ms, browsers wrap to an immediate fire.

So the "far future" timer fires right away — a genuinely surprising production bug when scheduling, say, 30-day expirations with raw timeouts.

For long horizons, store a deadline timestamp and re-schedule in shorter hops, or use a proper job scheduler.

questions.title: A microtask schedules a timer while one is already pending. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `micro, existing timer` — a timer set inside a microtask is dropped

questions.explanation:
The microtask runs first (before any macrotask) and logs `micro`. The timer it schedules joins the macrotask queue **behind** the already-pending `existing timer`, since both have 0ms delays and the existing one was registered earlier.

So: `micro` → `existing timer` → `timer from micro`.

General rule for mixed scheduling: a microtask can delay *when* you ask for a macrotask, but it can't jump the macrotask queue — new tasks always join at the back of their queue at the moment they're scheduled.

questions.title: What is the key behavioral difference between `setInterval(fn, 100)` and a recursive `setTimeout(fn, 100)`?

options.text: setInterval schedules on a fixed cadence regardless of how long `fn` takes; recursive setTimeout guarantees ~100ms of quiet between runs
There is no difference; setInterval is implemented as recursive setTimeout
setInterval callbacks are microtasks, setTimeout callbacks are macrotasks
Recursive setTimeout drifts, while setInterval is guaranteed drift-free

questions.explanation:
`setInterval` aims for a fixed cadence: the next firing is scheduled relative to the *previous deadline*, not to when your callback finished. If `fn` takes 80ms, only ~20ms separates runs — and if `fn` runs longer than the interval, executions can bunch up back-to-back (browsers skip intermediate firings rather than queueing them).

Recursive `setTimeout` schedules the next run *after* the current one completes, guaranteeing a minimum gap — usually what you actually want for polling.

Neither is drift-free: both are subject to event-loop delays and clamping; for wall-clock accuracy, compute against `Date.now()` each tick.

questions.title: A `for await` loop over a synchronous-looking async generator. In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
Even though the generator yields plain numbers with no real async work, the **async iteration protocol** wraps every `next()` result in a promise — and `for await...of` awaits each one. The first `await` suspends `asyncForOf`, so `after` logs before any value.

Each iteration then resumes as its own microtask: `val 1`, `val 2`, `val 3`, `done`.

Guaranteed minimum of one microtask tick per iteration is the protocol's price for uniformity: consumers never need to know whether the source is sync-fast or genuinely async.

questions.title: The generator awaits a timer between yields. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `sync, v 1, v 3, v 2, done` — the slow yield is reordered

questions.explanation:
An async generator may freely `await` between yields; the consuming loop simply waits longer for that `next()` promise. Values are **always delivered in yield order** — `v 2` arrives ~10ms late but never out of order, because the loop doesn't request the third value until the second has been consumed.

This demand-driven, one-at-a-time flow is the core of async iteration: backpressure is built in, since the producer only advances when the consumer asks.

Contrast with `Promise.all`, which starts everything eagerly and delivers all at once.

questions.title: `for await` over an array of promises with mixed timing. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `a, b, c` — input order, waiting for each in turn
A TypeError — for await requires an async iterable

questions.explanation:
`for await...of` also accepts a plain (sync) iterable of promises: it takes them **in input order** and awaits each before moving on. `c` is ready long before `b`, but the loop doesn't look at it until `b` has resolved.

So this construct is sequential consumption, not a "fastest first" stream. Total wait here is governed by the slowest *prefix*, like `await`ing one by one.

Note all three promises are already running concurrently (they were created up front) — only the *consumption* is serialized.

questions.title: What does the `next()` method of an **async iterator** return?

options.text: A promise that fulfills with `{ value, done }`
A `{ value, done }` object synchronously, like a sync iterator
A promise that fulfills with just the value
A `{ value: Promise, done: boolean }` object synchronously

questions.explanation:
The async iteration protocol wraps the *entire result object* in a promise: `next()` returns `Promise<{ value, done }>`. Even `done` is unknown until the promise settles — the consumer can't know whether iteration ended without awaiting.

This is why `for await` must suspend on every step, and why the "synchronous-looking async generator" still ticks through microtasks.

Compare the rejected design alternative `{ value: Promise, done: boolean }`: it couldn't represent "I don't know yet whether there's a next item", which streams genuinely need.
questions.references: [MDN: Iteration protocols#the async iterator and async iterable protocols](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Iteration_protocols#the_async_iterator_and_async_iterable_protocols)

questions.title: Sequential awaiting vs `Promise.all`. Which label logs first?

options.text: Both log at the same time with the same values
`parallel` logs values in completion order: `20,40,60` vs sequential's `60,40,20`

questions.explanation:
Both produce the same values, but timing differs sharply:

- **sequential** starts each timer only after the previous resolves: 10 + 20 + 30 ≈ 60ms total.
- **parallel** creates all three promises up front via `map`, so the timers run concurrently: total ≈ max(10, 20, 30) = 30ms.

Being called first doesn't help `sequential` — it spends most of its life suspended.

The `await`-in-a-loop pattern is right when each step *depends* on the previous; for independent work, build the promises first and `Promise.all` them.

questions.title: In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
The loop hits `await` *before* the first log, suspending `processAll` — so `sync` wins. Then each iteration resumes as one microtask: log the item, loop around, suspend again.

All three items (and `done`) drain in a single microtask cascade before any macrotask could run, since `Promise.resolve()` never touches the timer queue.

Note how `for...of` + `await` differs from the `forEach` pitfall: here the loop genuinely waits each iteration, preserving order and letting `done` come last — `forEach` would have fired everything and logged `done` first.

questions.title: A promise-based `sleep`. In what order do the values log?
questions.explanation:
`sleep` wraps `setTimeout` in a promise — the standard way to pause an async function. `main` logs `A`, suspends on a timer-backed promise, and the script logs `D`.

Each `await sleep(0)` yields all the way to the **macrotask** queue: the timer must fire, resolve the promise, and only then does a microtask resume `main` (`B`, then the cycle repeats for `C`).

That full event-loop round trip is what distinguishes `await sleep(0)` from `await Promise.resolve()` — the next question makes the contrast explicit.

questions.title: Microtask yield vs macrotask yield. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `macro yield, micro yield, timer` — macro() was called first

questions.explanation:
`macro()` runs first and registers its 0ms timer (timer #1); `micro()` suspends on an already-resolved promise; the script registers timer #2.

Drain order: the microtask resumes `micro` first (`micro yield`). Then timer #1 fires, resolving `macro`'s promise — its continuation is a microtask that runs before the next macrotask (`macro yield`). Finally timer #2 (`timer`).

Choose deliberately: `await Promise.resolve()` (or `queueMicrotask`) yields only to other microtasks; `await sleep(0)` lets timers, I/O, and rendering breathe. They are different tools, not interchangeable.

questions.title: A callback API is wrapped in a promise. In what order do the values log?

options.text: `sync` only — resolve inside a nested callback has no effect
`sync, got null` — the first callback argument wins

questions.explanation:
This is **promisification**: adapting a Node-style `(err, data)` callback API into a promise. The executor starts the legacy operation; the callback later routes failure to `reject` and success to `resolve`. Synchronous code (`sync`) runs first as always.

The error-first convention maps cleanly: `err ? reject(err) : resolve(data)` — after which the consumer composes with `await`, `.then`, and combinators like any modern API.

Node automates this exact transform with `util.promisify`; since promises settle once, any duplicate callback invocations are safely ignored.

questions.title: How many times is `flaky` called in total?
questions.explanation:
The retry loop calls `fn`, and `return await fn()` exits the **whole function** on the first success. Attempts 1 and 2 throw (caught and absorbed by the loop), attempt 3 returns `"ok"` — so `flaky` runs exactly **3** times; the remaining 2 permitted attempts never happen.

Note the `await` inside `try`: with plain `return fn()` the rejection would escape *past* the try/catch (the function has already returned), and no retry would ever occur. This is the classic case where `return await` is not redundant but load-bearing.

questions.title: `map` with an async callback. What logs?

options.text: `true false`, then `2,4,6` — map unwraps async results
`false true` — map over async callbacks returns a promise

questions.explanation:
An async callback always returns a promise, and `map` stores return values as-is — so `results` is a plain array of three `Promise` objects, not numbers (`true true`).

`Promise.all` is the canonical second step: it awaits them all and restores plain values (`2,4,6`).

`array.map(asyncFn)` → `Promise.all(...)` is the standard parallel-processing idiom; forgetting the `Promise.all` and treating `results[0]` as a number is the corresponding standard bug.

questions.title: Both operations are started **before** the first `await`. What logs?

options.text: `a b concurrent` — both timers ran simultaneously (~30ms total)
`a b sequential` — two awaits always mean ~60ms
A deadlock — pb cannot resolve while pa is being awaited

questions.explanation:
The work starts when the **promise is created**, not when it's awaited. Both `delay` calls run their timers concurrently from the first two lines; by the time `await pb` executes (after ~30ms of waiting on `pa`), `pb` is already settled and resumes in a tick.

Total: ~30ms, not ~60ms — hence `concurrent`.

The "create first, await later" pattern gets you parallelism without `Promise.all`, though `Promise.all([pa, pb])` is still preferred: it fails fast and avoids unhandled-rejection windows if `pb` rejects while you're still awaiting `pa`.

questions.title: After the race is decided, what happens to the slow promise?

options.text: `winner fast, slow finished` — the loser keeps running
`winner fast` only — losing a race cancels the promise
`winner fast, winner slow` — race settles once per input

questions.explanation:
`Promise.race` settles with the first result, but it has **no power over the inputs**: `slow`'s timer still fires at 30ms, runs its side effects (`slow finished`), and resolves a promise nobody reads anymore.

JavaScript promises represent results, not controllable operations — there is no built-in cancellation.

In real systems this means losing branches keep holding sockets, CPU, and locks. Pair racing with `AbortController` so the loser can actually stop working when the race is over.

questions.title: An in-flight `fetch` is aborted via `AbortController`. What happens to its promise?

options.text: It fulfills with a partial response

questions.explanation:
`AbortController` is the platform's standard cancellation primitive. Calling `abort()` makes `fetch` stop the network work and **reject** its promise with a `DOMException` whose `name` is `"AbortError"` — cancellation arrives through the normal rejection channel.

Idiomatic handling distinguishes it from real failures: `if (e.name === "AbortError") return;` in the catch.

The same `signal` is accepted by many APIs (`addEventListener`, streams, some libraries), and `AbortSignal.timeout(ms)` builds deadline signals directly — composing cancellation where bare promises cannot.

questions.title: Which statements about async/await are **true**? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: An async function always returns a promise
`try/catch` can handle a rejection from an awaited promise
`await` suspends only the async function itself, not the whole thread
`await` blocks the main thread until the promise settles

questions.explanation:
async/await is syntax over promises, not a new concurrency model:

- The return value is always wrapped in a promise (even bare `return;` → promise of `undefined`).
- `await` rethrows rejections at the await site, so ordinary `try/catch` works.
- Suspension is **cooperative**: the function parks itself and the event loop keeps running everything else.

The blocking claim is the classic misconception — nothing in JavaScript blocks the thread while waiting on a promise; only synchronous code (like a busy-wait loop) can do that.

questions.title: The grand finale — every mechanism at once. In what order do the numbers log?
questions.explanation:
Sync phase: `1`, the IIFE runs to its first `await` (`3`), then `10`. Queue: `[resume@4, then7, qm9]`; timer `2` waits in the macrotask queue.

Drain: `resume@4` logs `4`, enqueues `5`, suspends again (enqueues `resume@6`) → queue `[then7, qm9, 5, resume@6]`. `7` runs and only now enqueues chained `8`. Then `9`, `5`, `6`, `8`. Macrotask last: `2`.

Everything from this quiz in one trace: sync-first, await suspension points, FIFO microtasks, chains enqueueing lazily, and the macrotask running only after the queue is fully drained. (Verified by execution.)
